title: House of Many Ways - Chapter Five (Fragment 5)
text:
“Fell off the—Then why aren’t you dead?” Peter demanded. He backed away from her slightly, as if he thought she might be some kind of zombie.

“I worked a spell,” Charmain told him, rather airily because she was so proud of having worked real magic. “A flying spell.”

“Really?” said Peter, half eager, half suspicious. “What flying spell? Where?”

“Out of a book in here,” Charmain said. “And when I fell, I started to float and came down quite safely in the garden path. There’s no need to look so disbelieving. There was a kobold called Rollo in the garden when I came down. Ask him, if you don’t believe me.”

“I will,” Peter said. “What was this book? Show me.”

Charmain tossed her plait haughtily across her shoulder and went over to the desk. The Boke of Palimpsest seemed to be trying to hide. It was certainly not where she had left it. Perhaps Peter had moved it. She found it in the end, squeezed in among the row of Res Magica, pretending to be another volume of the encyclopedia. “There,” she said, banging it down on top of Hex, “and how dare you doubt my word! Now I’m going to find a book to read.”

She marched to one of the bookshelves and began picking out likely titles. None of the books seemed to have stories in them, which Charmain would have preferred, but some of the titles were quite interesting. What about The Thaumaturge as Artist, for instance, or Memoirs of an Exorcist ? On the other hand, The Theory and Practice of Choral Invocation looked decidedly dry, but Charmain rather fancied the one next to it, called The Twelve-Branched Wand.

Peter meanwhile sat himself down at the desk and leafed eagerly through The Boke of Palimpsest. Charmain was just discovering that The Thaumaturge as Artist was full of off-putting sayings like “thus our happy little magician can bring a sweet, fairylike music to our ears,” when Peter said irritably, “There’s no spell for flying in here. I’ve looked right through.”

“Perhaps I used it up,” Charmain suggested vaguely. She took a look inside The Twelve-Branched Wand and found it a very promising read.

“Spells don’t work like that,” Peter said. “Where did you find it, really?”

“In there. I told you,” Charmain said. “And if you can’t believe a word I say, why do you keep asking me?” She dropped her glasses off her nose, snapped the book shut, and carried a whole pile of likely volumes out into the corridor, where she slammed the study door on Peter and marched off backward and forward through the bathroom door until she reached the living room. There, in spite of the mustiness, she decided to stay. After that entry in Res Magica, outside in the sun did not seem safe anymore. She thought of the lubbock looming above the hydrangeas and sat herself firmly down on the sofa instead.

She was deep in The Twelve-Branched Wand , and even beginning to understand what it was about, when there was a sharp rapping at the front door. Charmain thought, just as she usually did, Someone else can answer that, and read on.

The door opened with an impatient rattle. Aunt Sempronia’s voice said, “Of course she’s all right, Berenice. She just has her nose in a book, as usual.”

Charmain tore herself out of the book and snatched off her glasses in time to see her mother following Aunt Sempronia into the house. Aunt Sempronia, as usual, was most impressively clothed in stiff silk. Mrs. Baker was at her most respectable in gray, with shining white collar and cuffs, and wore her most respectable gray hat.

vocabulary.definition: To strike something forcefully causing a loud noise.
vocabulary.example: “And when I fell, I started to float and came down quite safely in the garden path.

vocabulary.definition: Wearing clothes or having clothes on.
vocabulary.example: Aunt Sempronia, as usual, was most impressively clothed in stiff silk.

vocabulary.definition: In a definite and unmistakable way.
vocabulary.example: On the other hand, The Theory and Practice of Choral Invocation looked decidedly dry, but Charmain rather fancied the one next to it, called The Twelve-Branched Wand.

vocabulary.definition: To ask for something forcefully or with authority.
vocabulary.example: ” Peter demanded.

vocabulary.definition: Showing doubt or lack of belief.
vocabulary.example: There’s no need to look so disbelieving.

vocabulary.definition: A feeling of uncertainty or lack of conviction.
vocabulary.example: “There,” she said, banging it down on top of Hex, “and how dare you doubt my word!

vocabulary.definition: A comprehensive reference work containing articles on various subjects.
vocabulary.example: She found it in the end, squeezed in among the row of Res Magica, pretending to be another volume of the encyclopedia.

vocabulary.definition: A person who expels evil spirits from a person or place.
vocabulary.example: What about The Thaumaturge as Artist, for instance, or Memoirs of an Exorcist ?

vocabulary.definition: To rest or move on the surface of a liquid without sinking.
vocabulary.example: “And when I fell, I started to float and came down quite safely in the garden path.

vocabulary.definition: In a proud or disdainful manner.

”

Charmain tossed her plait haughtily across her shoulder and went over to the desk.

vocabulary.definition: Showing annoyance or restlessness because of delay.
vocabulary.example: The door opened with an impatient rattle.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner that evokes admiration.
vocabulary.example: Aunt Sempronia, as usual, was most impressively clothed in stiff silk.

vocabulary.definition: A mythical sprite or goblin in Germanic folklore.
vocabulary.example: There was a kobold called Rollo in the garden when I came down.

vocabulary.definition: Probable or expected.

”

She marched to one of the bookshelves and began picking out likely titles.

vocabulary.definition: A stale, moldy smell.
vocabulary.example: There, in spite of the mustiness, she decided to stay.

vocabulary.definition: A manuscript or piece of writing material on which the original writing has been effaced to make room for later writing.
vocabulary.example: The Boke of Palimpsest seemed to be trying to hide.

vocabulary.definition: To act as if something is true when it is not.
vocabulary.example: She found it in the end, squeezed in among the row of Res Magica, pretending to be another volume of the encyclopedia.

vocabulary.definition: Showing signs of future success.
vocabulary.example: She took a look inside The Twelve-Branched Wand and found it a very promising read.

vocabulary.definition: A sharp knocking sound.
vocabulary.example: She was deep in The Twelve-Branched Wand , and even beginning to understand what it was about, when there was a sharp rapping at the front door.

vocabulary.definition: Regarded by society as being good or acceptable.
vocabulary.example: Baker was at her most respectable in gray, with shining white collar and cuffs, and wore her most respectable gray hat.

vocabulary.definition: A form of words used as a magical charm or incantation.
vocabulary.example: “I worked a spell,” Charmain told him, rather airily because she was so proud of having worked real magic.

vocabulary.definition: The names of books, articles, or other works.

She marched to one of the bookshelves and began picking out likely titles.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that is uncertain or unclear.

”

“Perhaps I used it up,” Charmain suggested vaguely.

vocabulary.definition: A fictional reanimated corpse or mindless person.
vocabulary.example: He backed away from her slightly, as if he thought she might be some kind of zombie.
